data.content:
Since you already have the `Microsoft.Testing.Extensions.HotReload` package, you can run your tests in hot-reload mode with:

```
dotnet test --hotreload
```

This keeps the test host running and watches for code changes. When you save a file, it automatically re-runs your tests without a full rebuild — much faster iteration.

**Your current failing test:** `Validate_MissingAtSign_ReturnsFalse` fails because `IsValidEmail` only checks for null/empty but never checks for `@`. To fix, you'd edit `IsValidEmail` in your test file, save, and hot-reload will instantly re-run.

Want me to start a hot-reload session and fix the `IsValidEmail` method?
data.reasoningText:
The user wants to know how to use HotReload to iterate on fixes. Let me explain the workflow.

The project already has `Microsoft.Testing.Extensions.HotReload` referenced. The hot reload feature allows you to run tests in watch mode so that when you edit code, the tests automatically re-run without a full rebuild.

The way to use it is with `dotnet test --hotreload` or by running `dotnet watch test`.

Actually, let me think about this more carefully. The `Microsoft.Testing.Extensions.HotReload` package works with the Microsoft Testing Platform. When you run your tests with `--hotreload`, it keeps the test process running and watches for code changes, re-running tests when files change.


So I'd run `dotnet test --hotreload` to start the hot reload workflow, which will keep watching for changes and automatically re-run the tests as I make edits.
